summary:
The specifici ty of the PET radiotracer is determined by dividing the number of correct negative results (true negatives) among people without Parkinson’s disease by the total number of such individuals. With 580 true negatives and 20 false positives across 600 unaffected patients, specificity equals $ \frac{580}{600} $. This value corresponds directly to option D based on standard diagnostic criteria.

Final Answer:

\boxed{\text{D}}

Okay, let me now summarize only what was actually reasoned about during Round Two—without adding anything beyond it—and keep strictly within two sentences focusing solely on clinically significant elements related to evaluating performance of diagnostics like this tracer.

In diagnosing Parkinson’s disease, specificity measures how well the PET radiotracer identifies truly healthy individuals as negative; here, 580 out of 600 asymptomatic participants showed no positivity despite having no illness—a high rate indicating reliable exclusion when there isn’t actual pathology present. Thus, given these numbers derived from patient outcomes under controlled conditions, the best representation of its ability to avoid misdiagnoses aligns precisely with definition-based computation yielding $\dfrac{580}{600}$ which selects option D.
